artifacts.content:
Hi Hannah,

I finally have a quiet-ish minute while the kids are glued to Bluey and Daniel’s tinkering with the grill, so I wanted to actually say thank you for forwarding me that October newsletter from Sue last fall.

You were absolutely right that the “paper clutter costing you money” article could have been written about me. I still cringe when I think about March 2023 and that SeaBloom Organics project I had to cancel because I lost the signed contract and product brief somewhere in the black hole of my home office. Finding them three weeks after the deadline, buried under kids’ art and old client folders, was like a punch in the gut. That phone call at the La Jolla office with the owner, trying to explain why I’d dropped the ball, and then the 30% reduction in my monthly hours for six months… I don’t ever want to repeat that.

Between that and the late fees on bills and school forms getting lost in piles on the dining table, it finally pushed me to reach out to Sue. I’ve started blocking Monday mornings just for admin and paper triage. It’s not perfect, but it’s something. I even cleared enough off my desk that Ethan can sometimes use the corner for his Lego robotics stuff (when Lily isn’t “helping” with glitter).

How are things in Carlsbad? How’s work? If you’re free one weekend, maybe you could come down and help me decide what’s actually worth keeping from the kids’ artwork mountain. I trust your ruthless editor eye.

Love,
Melissa

metadata.subject: That newsletter you sent last fall

artifacts.content:
Mom,

I’ve been meaning to follow up after our budgeting talk at the house in Temecula in early January. I’m still a little mortified thinking about you sitting at the kitchen table with that Temecula Valley Federal Credit Union printout, highlighter in hand, circling every single late fee and overdraft charge from the last 12 months. Seeing it added up—over $1,200 just because I kept misplacing bills in the piles around my office and the dining room—was a real wake-up call.

I know you and Dad weren’t trying to shame me, but it did sting. In a good way, I guess, because it finally pushed me to do something instead of just saying, “I’ll get organized when things calm down.” Spoiler: things never calm down.

That same week I saw Sue Crum’s name pop up again in the San Diego Working Moms Facebook group. I remembered Jenna talking about how much she helped when she was drowning in paperwork after the baby, so I booked a Success Strategy Session with her. I was nervous about spending more money right after staring at that $1,200 number, but it feels more like preventing future dumb fees.

We’ve already set up a simple system: one basket by the front door for all incoming mail, and I have “Money Monday” on my calendar to actually open, sort, and pay things. Daniel is cautiously optimistic.

Thank you (really) for sitting down with me and not just rolling your eyes at my mess. Tell Dad I’m working on it.

Love,
Melissa

Hey Jenna,

Catching up between Zoom calls and snack requests. I was thinking about you the other day driving past Hera Hub in Mission Valley and remembering that meltdown in the parking lot back in September 2024. I can still picture myself sitting in the car, surrounded by unopened mail and crumpled Holmes Elementary forms, sobbing after that call about Ethan’s missing assignments and yet another ADHD behavior plan review.

I felt like the worst mom and the most unprofessional “professional” ever. You texting me that afternoon and then forwarding Sue’s newsletter honestly changed the trajectory a bit. Knowing you’d worked with her when you were going through postpartum depression and drowning in your own paperwork made it feel less like a personal failure and more like… a solvable problem.

Fast forward: I finally booked with Sue after that brutal Temecula money talk with my parents in early January—turns out $1,200 in late fees will motivate a girl. She’s helping me build systems so the car doesn’t become a traveling file cabinet again.

Work-wise, SeaBloom is steady. I’m still doing about 20 hours a week for them plus my own clients. I’m trying to keep Fridays lighter so I can actually show up for pizza-and-movie night without my laptop. Ethan’s band practice and Lily’s soccer schedule are a whole second job, but you know how that goes.

Let me know when you’re next at Hera Hub PB. I’d love a coworking day that doesn’t end in a panic attack.

xo,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Remember the Hera Hub parking lot?

artifacts.content:
Hi Dr. Lewis,

I wanted to give you a little context before our next session because the clutter/time stuff has really come to a head over the last couple of years.

Back in June 2024, Daniel got a written warning from HR at SunWave Solar for missing a safety training. The whole thing started because he couldn’t find the printed schedule in our bedroom, which has morphed into a shared “office” with piles of my client folders and the kids’ school papers on the floor and dresser. He’d left the schedule on the dresser, it got buried under one of my “temporary” stacks, and he completely spaced the training. After the Zoom call with his HR manager, he was furious and scared, and we had a huge argument about the state of the room.

He ended up sitting at the dining table with his laptop, Googling “San Diego home office organizer” while I was still crying in the bedroom. That’s how he found Sue Crum’s website and emailed me the link with the subject line, “We need this before I lose my job over a piece of paper.” At the time I felt attacked, but looking back, he was legitimately worried.

Layer that on top of my own mess—like the SeaBloom contract I lost in March 2023 that led to the 30% hours cut for six months, and the $1,200 in late fees my parents pointed out in January—and it’s clear this isn’t just about being “a bit messy.”

I’d like to use our sessions to untangle why I keep recreating these chaos zones even when the consequences are so high.

Best,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Some context on the clutter/work stress

artifacts.content:
Digital journal – 2025-01-07

I’m writing this while the house is finally quiet. Today feels like a turning point, or at least I want it to.

In the last couple of years, the clutter hasn’t just been annoying; it’s been actively sabotaging us. March 2023 was the first big “oh no” moment: losing that signed SeaBloom Organics contract and product brief in my home office, only finding them three weeks after the deadline. That led to canceling the project, the awful phone call at the La Jolla office, and the 30% cut in my hours for six months. We tightened our belts and I told myself I’d get systems in place.

Then June 2024: Daniel’s written warning from HR at SunWave Solar for missing that safety training because the schedule was buried under piles in our bedroom/office. The fight that night was brutal, and when he Googled “San Diego home office organizer” at the dining table and sent me Sue’s link with “before I lose my job over a piece of paper” in the subject, I felt both attacked and seen.

September 2024: panic attack in the Hera Hub Mission Valley parking lot after the call from Holmes about Ethan’s missing assignments and another ADHD behavior plan review. Sitting in the car surrounded by unopened mail and school forms, texting Jenna that I felt like a terrible mom. She replied later with Sue’s newsletter.

And then early January 2025: Mom and Dad at the Temecula kitchen table, highlighting over $1,200 in late fees and overdrafts from our Temecula Valley Federal Credit Union account because I keep losing bills.

Today I finally booked a Success Strategy Session with Sue. I’m scared, embarrassed, hopeful. Mostly hopeful.

metadata.title: Clutter rock-bottom timeline
metadata.location: 4271 Juniper Crest Way, San Diego, CA

artifacts.content:
Event: Success Strategy Session with Sue Crum – home office + paper systems

Date: Wednesday, 2025-01-22
Time: 10:00 a.m. – 11:30 a.m.
Location: Zoom from home office (clear at least one visible surface!)
Attendees: Melissa Carpenter, Sue Crum

Notes / Prep:
- This has been a long time coming. Remember: this is the same coach Jenna worked with when she was dealing with postpartum depression and drowning in paperwork. Jenna first sent me Sue’s newsletter after my panic attack in the Hera Hub Mission Valley parking lot in September 2024, when Holmes Elementary called about Ethan’s missing assignments and yet another ADHD behavior plan review while I was literally sitting in a car full of unopened mail.

- Also think about the money angle: the budgeting talk at Mom and Dad’s place in Temecula in early January, when Mom pulled out the Temecula Valley Federal Credit Union statement and highlighted over $1,200 in late fees and overdraft charges from the last 12 months. That’s what finally pushed me to book this.

- Don’t forget Daniel’s perspective: the June 2024 blow-up in our bedroom after his Zoom with HR at SunWave Solar about the written warning for missing the safety training because the printed schedule was buried under my piles.

Questions for Sue:
• Where to start: home office vs. dining table?
• Simple system for incoming paper (mail, school forms, receipts).
• How to time-block realistically with client work + SeaBloom + kids’ schedules.

Hey Prof. Daniels,

I hope you’re well and that SDSU isn’t drowning you in committee meetings. I’ve been thinking about your old “systems save creativity” lecture a lot lately.

The last couple of years have been a mess, literally and figuratively. In March 2023, I lost a big SeaBloom Organics project because I misplaced the signed contract and product brief in my home office. I only found them three weeks after the deadline, buried under sketch pads and the kids’ artwork. That led to a really uncomfortable call at their La Jolla office and a 30% reduction in my monthly hours for six months. It shook my confidence more than I admitted at the time.

Then there was June 2024, when my husband Daniel (he’s a project manager at SunWave Solar) got a written warning from HR for missing a safety training because the printed schedule disappeared into the piles in our bedroom/office. We had a huge argument, and he ended up Googling “San Diego home office organizer” and emailing me a link to a coach with the subject line, “We need this before I lose my job over a piece of paper.”

On top of that, my parents sat me down in Temecula this January and walked me through a Temecula Valley Federal Credit Union statement showing over $1,200 in late fees and overdraft charges from the last year. All because bills and notices get buried.

I finally hired that organizer/coach, and we’re building basic systems: inbox trays, weekly admin blocks, digital backups for contracts. It’s humbling to admit I needed help with the stuff you warned us about back in 2008, but better late than never.

If you ever want a cautionary tale for your students, I’m your girl.

Best,
Melissa
